title: Reverse linked list
description: Reverse a linked list using recursion

examples.explanation:
Start: prev=null, curr=1
Step 1: next=2, curr.next=null, prev=1, curr=2 (list: 1->null)
Step 2: next=3, curr.next=1, prev=2, curr=3 (list: 2->1->null)
Step 3: next=null, curr.next=2, prev=3, curr=null (list: 3->2->1->null)
Return prev=3 as new head

Start: prev=null, curr=5
Step 1: next=null, curr.next=null, prev=5, curr=null (list: 5->null)
Return prev=5 as new head

approaches.name: Approach 1: Iterative Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse the list, reversing links in place by updating next pointers. This is a straightforward iterative method that modifies the original list without extra data structures.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Linked List Iteratively
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of reversing a singly linked list using the two-pointer iterative approach.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is to traverse the list once while reversing the direction of each node's next pointer. We maintain three pointers: prev (initially null, will become the new head), curr (current node being processed), and next (temporarily stores the next node to move forward). At each step, we save curr.next to next, reverse curr.next to point to prev, then advance prev to curr and curr to next. This effectively flips each link in place. The algorithm runs in O(n) time as we visit each node once, and uses O(1) space since we only use a few pointers. This connects directly to the core logic of pointer manipulation. Common mistakes include losing reference to the next node before updating pointers (causing infinite loops) or not handling edge cases like empty lists. The real pattern involves careful pointer updates in a loop.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize prev = null, curr = head
2. While curr is not null:
   a. Store next = curr.next
   b. Reverse: curr.next = prev
   c. Advance: prev = curr, curr = next
3. Return prev (new head)

Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Start: prev=null, curr=1
Step 1: next=2, curr.next=null, prev=1, curr=2 (list: 1->null)
Step 2: next=3, curr.next=1, prev=2, curr=3 (list: 2->1->null)
Step 3: next=null, curr.next=2, prev=3, curr=null (list: 3->2->1->null)
Return prev=3 as new head

Start: prev=null, curr=5
Step 1: next=null, curr.next=null, prev=5, curr=null (list: 5->null)
Return prev=5 as new head

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse Links Between Two Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement the core operation of reversing the next pointer of a node to point to a previous node.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the iterative reversal. The intuition is that to reverse a linked list, we need to change each node's next pointer to point backward instead of forward. Given a current node and a previous node, we update current.next to point to previous. This single operation, when applied repeatedly across the list, builds the reversed structure. The algorithm involves: receiving curr and prev as inputs, performing curr.next = prev, and returning the updated nodes. It runs in O(1) time and space since it's a constant-time pointer assignment. This feeds into the full problem as the essential step inside the loop. Common mistakes include not saving the original curr.next before reversing (which loses the forward reference) or misunderstanding pointer relationships. The real pattern is a simple assignment that changes linkage.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: curr node, prev node
2. Reverse link: curr.next = prev
3. Output: updated curr (now pointing backward)

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Before: curr points to 3, prev is 1
Operation: set curr.next = 1
After: curr now points to 1 instead of 3

Before: curr points to null, prev is null
Operation: set curr.next = null
After: curr still points to null (no change as prev is null)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traversal with Pointer Advancement
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that traverses a linked list while advancing two pointers (prev and curr).
ladders.explanation:
The core insight is to move through the list systematically, updating pointers at each step to gradually reverse it. The intuition is that by keeping track of the previous node and current node, we can reverse links as we go. We start with prev at null (since the new tail will point to null) and curr at the head. In each iteration, we advance prev to curr and curr to the next node, effectively sliding a window of two nodes along the list. This traversal ensures we process each node exactly once. The algorithm runs in O(n) time as we traverse n nodes, with O(1) space for pointers. This connects to the full problem by providing the loop structure. Common mistakes include infinite loops from incorrect advancement or off-by-one errors. The real pattern is a while loop with careful pointer updates.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize prev = null, curr = head
2. While curr is not null:
   a. Advance prev = curr
   b. Advance curr = curr.next
3. End when curr becomes null

Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Start: prev=null, curr=1
Iter1: prev=1, curr=2
Iter2: prev=2, curr=3
Iter3: prev=3, curr=null
Loop ends

Start: prev=null, curr=a
Iter1: prev=a, curr=b
Iter2: prev=b, curr=null
Loop ends

ladders.title: Building Block — Saving Next Node Before Modification
ladders.desc: Practice storing the next node reference before changing the current node's next pointer.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept is crucial to avoid losing access to the rest of the list during reversal. The intuition is that when we reverse curr.next to point to prev, we overwrite the original forward reference. If we don't save it first, we cannot move to the next node. By storing curr.next in a temporary variable (next), we preserve the forward traversal path. This is a common pattern in linked list manipulation. The algorithm involves: given a current node, save its next pointer, then proceed with modifications. It runs in O(1) time and space. This feeds into the core logic by enabling safe pointer updates. Common mistakes include forgetting to save or saving after modification. The real pattern is a temporary variable assignment.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: curr node
2. Save next = curr.next
3. Output: next (the saved reference)

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

examples.input: Node with value 2, next pointer to node 3
examples.trace:
Given curr points to node 2, and curr.next points to node 3
Operation: next = curr.next (so next now points to node 3)
Result: next variable holds reference to node 3

examples.input: Node with value 5, next pointer null
examples.trace:
Given curr points to node 5, and curr.next is null
Operation: next = curr.next (so next becomes null)
Result: next variable is null

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node's Next Pointer
ladders.desc: Learn to access and use the next pointer of a linked list node.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation is about retrieving the reference stored in a node's next field. In a singly linked list, each node contains data and a next pointer that points to the following node (or null if it's the tail). The intuition is that this pointer is how we navigate the list. By accessing node.next, we can move to the next node in sequence. This is essential for traversal. The algorithm is simple: given a node, return its next pointer. It runs in O(1) time and space. This connects to saving the next node as it's the value being saved. Common mistakes include accessing next on a null node (causing errors) or misunderstanding that next is a reference. The real pattern is using dot notation to access attributes.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: node
2. Access next pointer: node.next
3. Output: the next node reference (or null)

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

examples.input: Node A with next pointer to Node B
examples.trace:
Given node A, where A.next points to B
Operation: access A.next
Result: returns the reference to node B

examples.input: Node X with next pointer null
examples.trace:
Given node X, where X.next is null
Operation: access X.next
Result: returns null

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building block of a singly linked list: a node with data and a next pointer.
ladders.explanation:
This is the simplest prerequisite: a linked list node. In a singly linked list, each node is an object that holds some data (like a number or string) and a pointer/reference called 'next' that points to the next node in the sequence. The intuition is that nodes are connected in a chain via these next pointers, forming the list. The last node's next points to null, indicating the end. This structure allows dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays. The algorithm involves defining a node class with attributes for data and next. It uses O(1) space per node. This feeds into all higher levels as the fundamental unit. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with the data they hold or not initializing next to null. The real pattern is a simple class or struct.

Step-by-step concept:
1. A node has two parts: data (stores value) and next (stores reference to next node)
2. Example: Node with data=5, next=null is a single-node list
3. Multiple nodes link via next pointers

Time: O(1) to create, Space: O(1) per node

examples.trace:
Define a node object
Set its data attribute to 10
Set its next attribute to null (default for a single node)
Result: a node ready to be part of a list

examples.input: Link two nodes: node1 with data=1, node2 with data=2
examples.trace:
Create node1 with data=1, next initially null
Create node2 with data=2, next initially null
Set node1.next = node2
Now node1 points to node2, forming a list: 1 -> 2 -> null

approaches.name: Approach 2: Recursive Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively traverse to the end of the list, then unwind the call stack to reverse links by updating next pointers on the way back. This leverages the system call stack for reversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Linked List Using Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that reverses a singly linked list by traversing to the end and updating pointers during stack unwinding.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the system call stack to reverse a linked list. The intuition is that by recursively reaching the end of the list, we can work backwards through the call stack to reverse the links between nodes. When we reach the base case (null or single node), we start returning nodes up the call stack. During each return, we reverse the direction of the next pointer for the current node. The key insight is that the recursive call gives us access to both the current node and the next node in the original order, allowing us to flip their relationship. The algorithm works by: 1) Recursively calling reverse on head.next until reaching the end, 2) When unwinding, setting head.next.next = head to reverse the link, 3) Setting head.next = null to prevent cycles, 4) Returning the new head (which was originally the last node). Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) due to the recursive call stack. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive reversal function. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases (empty list, single node), forgetting to set head.next = null which creates cycles, and misunderstanding the return value (should be the new head from deepest recursion).

examples.trace: Step 1: reverse(1) calls reverse(2). Step 2: reverse(2) calls reverse(3). Step 3: reverse(3) calls reverse(null). Step 4: reverse(null) returns null. Step 5: reverse(3) returns 3 (base case, single node). Step 6: Unwind to reverse(2): head=2, head.next=3, set head.next.next=head makes 3->2, set head.next=null breaks original 2->3, return new_head=3. Step 7: Unwind to reverse(1): head=1, head.next=2, set head.next.next=head makes 2->1, set head.next=null breaks original 1->2, return new_head=3. Final: 3->2->1->null.
Step 1: reverse(5) calls reverse(null). Step 2: reverse(null) returns null. Step 3: reverse(5) returns 5 (base case: head.next is null, so return head). Final: 5->null unchanged.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Reversal Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that traverses to the end of the list and reverses pointers during return.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the recursive reversal approach. The intuition is that by making a recursive call on head.next, we defer the reversal work until we reach the end of the list. When we finally start returning from the deepest call, we have access to consecutive nodes in their original order, allowing us to reverse their relationship. The function takes a node as input and returns the new head of the reversed list. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head (empty list or single node). 2) Recursive case: call reverse(head.next) and store the result as new_head. 3) Reverse the link: set head.next.next = head (makes the next node point back to current). 4) Break the original link: set head.next = null. 5) Return new_head. Time complexity is O(n) for n recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack. This connects to L0 as the main function that solves the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling, not storing the result of the recursive call, and modifying pointers in the wrong order. The key pattern is that the recursive call happens before pointer manipulation, creating a post-order traversal effect.

examples.input: Node A with next=B, B next=null
examples.trace: Step 1: reverse(A) called. Step 2: Base case check: A.next=B not null, so proceed. Step 3: Call reverse(B). Step 4: reverse(B): B.next is null, so return B (base case). Step 5: Back in reverse(A): new_head=B. Step 6: Set A.next.next=A: B.next=A. Step 7: Set A.next=null. Step 8: Return new_head=B. Final: B points to A, A points to null.
Step 1: reverse(X) called. Step 2: Base case: X.next is null, so return X immediately. No pointer manipulation needed. Final: X->null unchanged.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer Reversal During Stack Unwinding
ladders.desc: The mechanism of reversing node links while returning from recursive calls.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that makes recursive reversal work. The intuition is that the call stack preserves the order of nodes, so when we unwind, we process nodes in reverse order. During each return from a recursive call, we have access to two adjacent nodes: the current node (head) and the node that was originally next (head.next). By making head.next point back to head, we reverse their relationship. The critical operations are: head.next.next = head (reverse the forward link) and head.next = null (break the original link to prevent cycles). This works because when we return from reverse(head.next), we know that everything beyond head.next is already reversed, so we just need to attach head to the new reversed sublist. The algorithm for this step: 1) After recursive call returns new_head, 2) Set head.next.next = head, 3) Set head.next = null, 4) Return new_head (which remains unchanged). Time complexity for this step is O(1) per node, but overall O(n). Space complexity involves the stack. This connects to L1 as the pointer manipulation inside the recursive function. Common mistakes include doing pointer reversal before the recursive call (which would break traversal), not setting head.next to null (creating cycles), and misunderstanding that new_head doesn't change during unwinding. The pattern is strictly post-order: recurse first, then manipulate pointers.

examples.input: At node B during unwinding, with A as head and B as head.next (originally A->B->C... already reversed to ...->C->B)
examples.trace: Step 1: We're in reverse(A) after reverse(B) returned new_head=C. Step 2: Current state: A.next=B, B.next=null (since reverse(B) set it). Step 3: Do head.next.next=head: B.next=A (now C->B->A). Step 4: Do head.next=null: A.next=null. Step 5: Return new_head=C. Now C->B->A->null.

examples.input: At node X during unwinding, single node case
examples.trace: Step 1: reverse(X) called with X.next=null. Step 2: Base case returns X immediately, no pointer manipulation occurs. The pointer reversal logic is skipped entirely.

testCases.input: head=A, head.next=B, after reverse(B) returned C
head=P, head.next=Q, after reverse(Q) returned R
head=M, head.next=N, after reverse(N) returned O
head=alpha, head.next=beta, after reverse(beta) returned gamma
head=node1, head.next=node2, after reverse(node2) returned node3
head=first, head.next=second, after reverse(second) returned third
head=A, head.next=B, after reverse(B) returned B (single node)

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Traversal to List End
ladders.desc: Recursively traverse a linked list until reaching the end (null pointer).
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using recursion to visit every node until the termination condition. The intuition is that a linked list is a recursive structure: each node points to a smaller sublist. By calling the function on head.next, we move one step deeper into the list. The base case occurs when head is null (empty list) or head.next is null (last node). This traversal doesn't modify the list; it just reaches the end. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head. 2) Recursive case: return traverse(head.next). Time complexity is O(n) as we make n recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack. This connects to L2 as the first part of the reversal process—we must reach the end before we can start reversing pointers on the way back. Common mistakes include infinite recursion (not updating head in recursive call), incorrect base case (missing null check), and not returning the result of the recursive call. This is essentially a recursive version of a while loop that goes to the end. The pattern is simple recursion with tail recursion if optimized, but we need the calls to stack up for later unwinding.

examples.trace: Step 1: traverse(1) calls traverse(2). Step 2: traverse(2) calls traverse(3). Step 3: traverse(3): head.next is null, so return 3 (base case). Step 4: traverse(2) returns 3. Step 5: traverse(1) returns 3. Final result is node 3.
Step 1: traverse(A): head.next is null, so return A immediately (base case). No further recursion.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Pointer Manipulation
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of updating next pointers between linked list nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation in linked list reversal: changing where a node's next pointer points to. In a singly linked list, each node has a data field and a next pointer. To reverse the list, we need to redirect these pointers backward. The intuition is that by changing node.next from pointing forward to pointing backward, we invert the list direction. The key operations are: 1) Accessing node.next to get the next node, 2) Setting node.next to a new node (or null). For example, if we have A->B->C and want B->A, we do B.next = A. But we must be careful to not lose reference to C. In the recursive approach, we use head.next.next = head to make the next node point back. This operation alone doesn't reverse the whole list; it reverses one link at a time. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as the action performed during stack unwinding. Common mistakes include dereferencing null pointers, creating cycles by incorrect assignments, and not updating pointers in the correct order. The pattern involves temporary variables if needed, but in recursion, the call stack provides the necessary references.

examples.input: Node A with next=B, Node B with next=C
examples.output: B.next changed from C to A
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with A.next=B, B.next=C. Step 2: To make B point to A, execute B.next = A. Step 3: Now A.next=B, B.next=A, C is disconnected. Step 4: Typically we'd also set A.next=null later to complete reversal.

examples.input: Node X with next=Y, Y next=null
examples.output: Y.next changed from null to X
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with X.next=Y, Y.next=null. Step 2: Execute Y.next = X. Step 3: Now X.next=Y, Y.next=X, creating a cycle X->Y->X. Step 4: Must also set X.next=null to break the cycle and get Y->X->null.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: The basic building block of a singly linked list: a node with data and a next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes. Each node contains two parts: data (the value) and next (a pointer/reference to the next node). The intuition is that nodes are connected in a chain via these next pointers. The last node's next points to null, indicating the end. For example, a list 1->2->3 has three nodes: node1 with data=1, next=node2; node2 with data=2, next=node3; node3 with data=3, next=null. This structure allows dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays. In the context of reversal, we only need to manipulate these next pointers; the data stays unchanged. Time and space for a single node are O(1). This connects to L4 as the object we perform pointer manipulation on. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their data, not understanding that next is a reference (like a link), and forgetting about the null terminator. For beginners, visualize it as a treasure hunt where each clue points to the next location.

examples.trace: Step 1: Define a Node class with attributes data and next. Step 2: Instantiate a node: node = Node(5, null). Step 3: The node has data=5, next=null. It's a single node list.

examples.input: Link two nodes: A with data=1 and B with data=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node A: data=1, next=null. Step 2: Create node B: data=2, next=null. Step 3: Set A.next = B. Step 4: Now A points to B, B points to null. The list is A->B->null.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Tail Recursive O(n)
approaches.summary: Implement recursion with an accumulator parameter to reverse links in a tail-recursive manner, avoiding extra stack overhead in languages that optimize tail calls. This builds the reversed list incrementally during recursion.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) in non-optimized languages, O(1) in optimized ones

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Linked List Using Tail Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of reversing a singly linked list using tail recursion with accumulator parameter.
ladders.explanation: This solution reverses a linked list using tail recursion, which builds the reversed list incrementally by passing an accumulator parameter. The intuition is that we can traverse the original list while constructing the reversed version simultaneously, avoiding the need to backtrack through the call stack. At each recursive step, we detach the current node from the original list and prepend it to the accumulator (which holds the partially reversed list). When we reach the end of the original list, the accumulator contains the fully reversed list. The algorithm uses two parameters: the current node being processed and the accumulator (reversed list so far). Starting with the head node and an empty accumulator, we recursively move through the list, building the reversed version. In languages with tail call optimization, this approach uses O(1) space; otherwise, it uses O(n) space for the call stack. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define a recursive function reverse_helper(current, accumulator) that returns the reversed list. 2. Base case: if current is None, return accumulator (the reversed list is complete). 3. Recursive case: store current.next in a temporary variable, set current.next to accumulator (prepend current to reversed list), then recursively call reverse_helper with the original next node as new current and current as new accumulator. 4. The main function calls reverse_helper(head, None). Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity depends on tail call optimization. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the tail recursive helper function as its core component. Common mistakes include not properly handling the next pointer before recursion (causing cycles), forgetting to return the accumulator in base case, or misunderstanding how the accumulator builds the reversed list. The pattern demonstrates how tail recursion can transform iterative accumulation into recursive form.

examples.trace:
Initial call: reverse_helper(1, None)
Step 1: current=1, acc=None, next_temp=2, 1.next=None, call reverse_helper(2, 1)
Step 2: current=2, acc=1, next_temp=3, 2.next=1, call reverse_helper(3, 2)
Step 3: current=3, acc=2, next_temp=4, 3.next=2, call reverse_helper(4, 3)
Step 4: current=4, acc=3, next_temp=5, 4.next=3, call reverse_helper(5, 4)
Step 5: current=5, acc=4, next_temp=None, 5.next=4, call reverse_helper(None, 5)
Step 6: current=None, return acc=5 (which points to 4->3->2->1->None)

Initial call: reverse_helper(10, None)
Step 1: current=10, acc=None, next_temp=20, 10.next=None, call reverse_helper(20, 10)
Step 2: current=20, acc=10, next_temp=None, 20.next=10, call reverse_helper(None, 20)
Step 3: current=None, return acc=20 (which points to 10->None)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Tail Recursive Helper Function with Accumulator
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive helper function that reverses links using accumulator parameter.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the tail recursive approach. The helper function takes two parameters: the current node being processed and an accumulator that holds the partially reversed list. The intuition is that we build the reversed list incrementally by prepending each node to the accumulator as we traverse forward. At each step, we detach the current node from its original next pointer and attach it to the front of the accumulator. This transforms the problem from "reverse the entire list" to "prepend current node to already-reversed remainder." The algorithm: 1. Base case: if current is None, return accumulator (the reversed list is complete). 2. Recursive case: store current.next in temp variable, set current.next to accumulator (prepend current to reversed list), then return recursive call with temp as new current and current as new accumulator. The key insight is that the accumulator always contains the reversed portion of nodes we've already processed. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for call stack without optimization, O(1) with tail call optimization. Connection to L0: This helper function is called by the main solution with initial parameters (head, None). Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of building reversed list incrementally. Common mistakes include modifying current.next before saving the original next pointer (losing reference to rest of list), not returning the recursive call result, or misunderstanding how the accumulator grows. The pattern shows how tail recursion with accumulator can replace iterative accumulation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, acc=None, next_temp=2, set 1.next=None, call helper(2, 1)
Step 2: current=2, acc=1, next_temp=None, set 2.next=1, call helper(None, 2)
Step 3: current=None, return acc=2 (which points to 1->None)

Step 1: current=5, acc=4->3, next_temp=None, set 5.next=4->3, call helper(None, 5)
Step 2: current=None, return acc=5 (which points to 4->3->None)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Building Reversed List Incrementally with Accumulator
ladders.desc: Understand how to construct reversed list by prepending nodes to accumulator during traversal.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains the fundamental technique of building the reversed list incrementally using an accumulator parameter. The intuition is that instead of reversing the entire list at once or using backtracking, we construct the reversed version piece by piece as we traverse forward. At each step, we take the current node and prepend it to the already-reversed portion (accumulator), then continue with the next node. This transforms the reversal into a linear accumulation process. The key insight is recognizing that reversing a list is equivalent to traversing it while building a new list where each new node becomes the new head. The algorithm: 1. Start with empty accumulator (representing empty reversed list). 2. For each node in original list: save its next pointer, make it point to accumulator (prepend), update accumulator to this node, move to saved next pointer. 3. When traversal completes, accumulator holds fully reversed list. This approach works because each prepend operation reverses the order: the first node becomes last, second becomes second-last, etc. Time complexity is O(n) with single pass. Space complexity is O(1) for variables (excluding recursion). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive helper function. Connection to L3: This builds upon understanding of linked list node manipulation. Common mistakes include not saving the next pointer before modifying current.next (causing loss of rest of list), incorrectly updating the accumulator, or misunderstanding the prepend operation. The pattern demonstrates how accumulation can solve reversal problems efficiently.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Process A: save next=B, A.next=None, acc=A
Step 2: Process B: save next=C, B.next=A, acc=B
Step 3: Process C: save next=None, C.next=B, acc=C
Result: C points to B points to A points to None

Step 1: Process 1: save next=2, 1.next=3->4, acc=1
Step 2: Process 2: save next=None, 2.next=1->3->4, acc=2
Result: 2 points to 1 points to 3 points to 4 points to None

ladders.title: Building Block — Manipulating Linked List Node Pointers
ladders.desc: Practice detaching and reattaching node pointers in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental skill of manipulating pointers in a linked list node. Each node typically has a value and a next pointer referencing the next node. The intuition is that reversing a list requires changing these next pointers to point in the opposite direction. The key operations are: 1. Accessing a node's next pointer (node.next). 2. Saving a reference to a node (temp = node.next). 3. Modifying a node's next pointer (node.next = new_target). 4. Traversing via next pointers (node = node.next). These operations allow us to detach nodes from their original positions and reattach them elsewhere in the list. For reversal specifically, we need to make each node point to its predecessor instead of its successor. The algorithm for a single node reversal step: given current node and previous node, save current.next to temp, set current.next to previous, then update previous to current and current to temp. This basic pointer manipulation is the mechanical foundation for the reversal process. Time complexity for single operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for temporary variables. Connection to L2: This pointer manipulation enables the incremental building of reversed list. Connection to L4: This builds upon the basic operation of accessing node attributes. Common mistakes include losing references by overwriting pointers before saving them, creating cycles by incorrect pointer assignments, or null pointer errors when accessing next of null node. The pattern is essential for any linked list algorithm.

examples.input: Node A: value=1, next=B; Node B: value=2, next=None; Make A point to None instead of B
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initially A.next = B
Step 2: Execute A.next = None
Step 3: Now A.next = None, B is no longer referenced by A

examples.input: Node X: value=10, next=Y; Node Y: value=20, next=Z; Make Y point to X instead of Z
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initially X.next=Y, Y.next=Z
Step 2: Save Y.next reference: temp=Z
Step 3: Set Y.next = X
Step 4: Now Y points to X instead of Z, Z is saved in temp

testCases.input: Node(5, next=Node(10)), set next to None
Node(a, next=Node(b)), set next to Node(c)
Node(100, next=None), set next to Node(200)
Node(x, next=Node(y)), save next to temp, set next to None
Node(7, next=Node(8)), make it point to Node(9)
Node(3, next=Node(4)), swap next with Node(5)
Node(0, next=None), modify next to itself
Node(m, next=Node(n)), detach n by setting m.next=None

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Node Attributes
ladders.desc: Learn how to read and write the value and next pointer of a linked list node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the fundamental actions of interacting with a linked list node's attributes. A node is typically a structure with at least two attributes: a value (data) and a next pointer (reference to next node). The intuition is that we need to both read these attributes to traverse the list and write them to modify the list structure. The key operations are: 1. Accessing node.value to read the data. 2. Accessing node.next to read the reference to next node. 3. Modifying node.value to change the data (though not needed for reversal). 4. Modifying node.next to change which node comes next. These operations are the atomic units of linked list manipulation. For example, to move to the next node, we do current = current.next. To insert a node after current, we do new_node.next = current.next; current.next = new_node. For reversal, we primarily modify next pointers. The algorithm for basic access: given a node object, use dot notation (node.attribute) to get or set attributes. Time complexity is O(1) per access. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: These operations enable the pointer manipulation needed for reversal. Connection to L5: This builds upon the concept of what a node is. Common mistakes include trying to access attributes of a null node (causing null pointer error), confusing node.value with node.next, or not understanding that node.next is a reference rather than a value. The pattern is universal across all linked list operations.

examples.input: Node with value=5, next=Node(10); Read value and next
examples.trace:
Step 1: Access node.value returns 5
Step 2: Access node.next returns reference to Node(10)
Step 3: Node(10) has value=10, next=None

examples.input: Node with value='a', next=None; Modify next to point to new Node('b')
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initially node.next = None
Step 2: Create new_node = Node('b', next=None)
Step 3: Execute node.next = new_node
Step 4: Now node.next points to Node('b')

testCases.input: Node(x, next=Node(y)), set next to null
Node(7, next=None), create and set next to Node(8)
Node(a, next=Node(b)), read value of next of next
Node(alpha, next=None), check if next is None

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Learn what a linked list node is and how it forms a chain.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic building block of a linked list: the node. A node is a simple data structure that contains two main parts: 1. A value (or data) that stores the actual information. 2. A next pointer (or reference) that points to the next node in the sequence. The intuition is that multiple nodes connected via their next pointers form a chain called a linked list. Unlike arrays where elements are stored contiguously in memory, linked list nodes can be scattered, with each node knowing only about its own value and the next node. This structure allows dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. A special case is the null pointer (often represented as None or null), which indicates the end of the list. For example, a list with nodes A->B->C has: Node A with value and next pointing to B, Node B with value and next pointing to C, Node C with value and next pointing to null. The algorithm for understanding: visualize each node as a box with two compartments (value and next), where next contains an arrow to the next box. Time and space complexity aren't applicable at this conceptual level. Connection to L4: This structure enables the attribute access operations. Connection to higher levels: This fundamental understanding is prerequisite for any linked list algorithm. Common mistakes include confusing the node with its value, not understanding that next is a reference/pointer rather than a value, or misunderstanding null termination. The pattern is the starting point for all linked list problems.

examples.input: Create a node with value=10 and no next node
examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for a node object
Step 2: Set node.value = 10
Step 3: Set node.next = None (null pointer)
Step 4: Result is a single node that points to nothing, representing a list with one element

examples.input: Connect Node(1) to Node(2) to form 1->2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create Node(1) with next=None
Step 2: Create Node(2) with next=None
Step 3: Set Node(1).next = Node(2)
Step 4: Now Node(1) points to Node(2), Node(2) points to None, forming a two-node list

testCases.input: How is end of list represented?

testCases.expected: Two boxes: first with value=5, next arrow to second; second with value=10, next=null

testCases.input: If node.next is None, what does it mean?
testCases.expected: It is the last node in the list

testCases.input: Can a node point to itself?
testCases.expected: Yes, by setting node.next = node, creating a cycle

testCases.input: What is the difference between node and node.value?
testCases.expected: node is the object, node.value is the data it contains

testCases.input: How many nodes in list: A->B->C?
What is the next of the last node in A->B->C?
Can a node have multiple next pointers?
testCases.expected: No, singly linked list node has exactly one next pointer

testCases.input: What happens if we lose reference to first node?
testCases.expected: We lose access to the entire list
